patches.find:
1.8 The Refraction Cascade: When One Fix Breaks Everything

The dome's own fix for the sun/firmament collision — splitting sun altitude into “apparent” (5,733 km) and “physical” (< 4,200 km) — creates a chain reaction that breaks eight other predictions. Any prediction that depends on the sun's physical position inherits the contradiction the fix was supposed to resolve.

Section 1.5 documents a geometric collision: the sun's triangulated altitude (5,733 km) exceeds the firmament height at the sun's orbital radius (~4,200 km). The dome fixes this by declaring the 5,733 km figure an "optical illusion due to aetheric refraction" (raw-text/05-model.txt, line 38) and asserting the physical sun sits below ~4,200 km. This section traces what that fix costs.

The physical altitude is not a single number

The firmament height function H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km gives different ceilings at different radii. At the Tropic of Cancer (r ≈ 7,400 km), H ≈ 3,618 km. At the Tropic of Capricorn (r ≈ 12,600 km), H ≈ 1,975 km. If the physical sun must stay below H(r) as it orbits, its altitude varies from ~3,618 km in northern summer to ~1,975 km in southern summer — an 83% swing. This is not a minor correction; it is a seasonal altitude oscillation of over 1,600 km that the dome has never documented, modelled, or tested.

The refraction required is extraordinary

To make a sun at 1,975–3,618 km appear at 5,733 km requires bending light by 6–29° depending on the observer's distance and the season (see Section 1.5 for the ceiling-case estimate of 6–9°). For comparison, the strongest atmospheric refraction on Earth — at the horizon — is 0.57°. The dome's own n(r) formula gives refractive indices of 1.2–2.8 across the disc, which would displace all light paths by multiple degrees, including starlight. Gaia DR3 measures stellar positions to ~10 microarcseconds (0.00000278°). A medium with n = 1.2 at the sun's orbital radius is more than six orders of magnitude too refractive for microarcsecond stellar astrometry.

Which predictions break?

The key distinction is between predictions that depend on the sun's apparent position (sky coordinates, analemma shape) and those that depend on its physical position (eclipse shadows, gravitational and tidal effects, H(r)-derived values). Apparent-position predictions see 5,733 km by construction — the fix preserves them. Physical-position predictions cannot use 5,733 km (the sun is not there) and cannot use a single physical altitude (it varies seasonally). These predictions inherit the contradiction.

WINClaimDepends onCascade Effect
056Solar elevation from H(r)PhysicalH(r) at the sun's radius gives the collision ceiling, not a solar elevation. Anchor WIN.
044Firmament Scaling FunctionPhysicalFSF is derived from H(r); seasonal physical altitude makes the derivation indeterminate.
046Winter solstice geometryPhysicalSolar orbit radius determines solstice geometry; physical altitude uncertainty propagates.
067Antarctic gravity holePhysicalH(r) at rim radii gives n(r) values that imply catastrophic gravity gradients never observed.
068Eclipse magnetic ensemblePhysicalEclipse shadow positions require a definite physical sun altitude; the cascade makes it indeterminate across 39 eclipses.
010BOU eclipse magnetic anomalyPhysicalSame eclipse geometry applies (shadow position indeterminate), but WIN-010 verdict is unaffected: the Chapman Sq mechanism concerns UV-flux-driven ionospheric current magnitude, independent of where the shadow falls. Supplementary cross-reference only — no verdict change.
016Stellar aberration modelPhysicaln(r) required for solar bending would displace all stellar positions by degrees (supplementary to Gaia refutation).
065Atmospheric scale heightPhysicalScale height derivation uses H(r); seasonal variation contaminates it (supplementary to data refutation).

Two of these eight WINs have their verdicts changed by the cascade: WIN-044 (Misleading → Self-Contradicted) and WIN-068 (Std Model Explains → Self-Contradicted). WIN-010 (BOU eclipse magnetic anomaly) is cross-referenced as supplementary: the cascade addresses eclipse shadow position indeterminacy, while WIN-010 concerns magnetic anomaly magnitude via the Chapman ionospheric mechanism — a mechanistically distinct argument unaffected by the physical altitude swing. The remaining five already carry correct verdicts; the cascade adds a cross-cutting structural argument that reinforces them.

The other side

The dome defender's strongest counter: apparent-position observations (analemma, crepuscular rays) are unaffected because the physical/apparent split preserves the apparent track by design. This is correct — and it is why the table above classifies every WIN by dependency type. We claim only predictions that require the physical altitude. The defender might also argue that a future n(r) derivation could specify the seasonal physical path. Perhaps — but until that derivation exists, any prediction that needs a definite physical sun position is using a value the model cannot provide.

1.8.1 Differential Refraction: The Eclipse Geometry Trap

TLDR: The dome's refraction fix shifts the sun's apparent position but never computes how the same medium shifts the moon. Since they're at different heights traversing different n(r) profiles, eclipse alignment — which requires precise angular overlap — becomes indeterminate. The dome's own OPEN-007 admits the moon's physical altitude is unknown.

Section 1.8 documents how the refraction fix creates a cascade of broken predictions for the sun. But the cascade has a second, independent dimension: the moon.

The dome model places the sun at an apparent altitude of 5,733 km and the moon at 2,534 km. Its own n(r) function — n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8537/H(r) − 1) — defines a spatially varying refractive medium throughout the cavity. At the equatorial ring (r ≈ 14,105 km), n = 1.40; at the ice wall (r = 20,015 km), n = 3.49. Light from any source traversing this medium follows curved paths, not straight lines.

The refraction fix was introduced for the sun: the 5,733 km triangulated altitude is declared an "optical illusion due to aetheric refraction" (model.html), with the physical sun sitting below the firmament ceiling (~4,200 km at the sun's orbital radius). But the same medium surrounds the moon. If n(r) bends sunlight, it bends moonlight — by a different amount, because the moon is at a different physical altitude and may be at a different radial distance, traversing a different n(r) profile along its optical path to the observer.

The logical trap

Before the refraction fix, the dome's eclipse geometry was calibrated with straight-line optics: sun physically at 5,733 km, moon physically at 2,534 km, geometric ray-tracing produces the correct angular overlap for eclipses. After the fix, neither body is where it appears:

  • The sun's physical altitude is unknown (constrained only to "below ~4,200 km" — a range spanning 1,975–3,618 km seasonally, as documented in Section 1.8).
  • The moon's physical altitude is explicitly unknown — OPEN-007 on the dome's own model page flags 2,534 km as "optical/apparent — physical altitude unknown" and labels the entire moon framework a "structural problem."

For a solar eclipse, the moon's physical body must intercept physical photons from the sun. The blocking geometry depends on where both bodies physically are, not where they appear. With curved light paths through n(r), the shadow cone's geometry changes: its axis follows the refracted ray bundle, its width depends on the physical sun-moon separation (not the apparent separation), and its ground intersection — the eclipse path — shifts accordingly.

The dome faces a trilemma:

  1. Keep straight-line eclipse geometry — but this contradicts the refraction fix. You cannot simultaneously claim n(r) bends sunlight (to resolve the firmament collision) and that n(r) doesn't bend the sun-moon-observer light path (to preserve eclipse geometry).
  2. Compute eclipse geometry through n(r) — but this requires knowing both physical altitudes (sun and moon) and performing full ray-tracing through a medium with n up to 3.49. The dome has done none of this. OPEN-007 means the moon's physical position is not even approximately known.
  3. Abandon the refraction fix — but then the sun physically exceeds the firmament height, and the Section 1.5 collision returns.

Scope of damage

This is not limited to eclipse shadow paths. Differential refraction affects every observation that compares the apparent positions of two bodies at different heights:

  • Eclipse shadow geometry (WIN-010, WIN-025, WIN-068, 2026 EBR prediction): The shadow path depends on physical sun-moon geometry through n(r). The dome's FSF formula scales magnetic predictions by station position relative to the shadow path — if the path is wrong, the scaling is wrong.
  • Lunar phase geometry: The apparent angular separation between sun and moon determines the illumination phase. Differential refraction shifts this separation, changing the predicted phase angle.
  • Tidal predictions (WIN-045, 049, 050, 051): Tidal forcing depends on the moon's physical altitude and distance, both unknown under n(r).
  • Lunar magnetic coupling (WIN-039): The predicted 1–2 nT signal depends on the moon's physical distance from the observer, which n(r) makes indeterminate.

The other side

A dome defender might argue that apparent-position alignment is sufficient for eclipses: if the moon appears to cover the sun from the observer's perspective, the eclipse occurs regardless of physical positions. This would be true in a model where all observations are purely optical. But eclipses have physical consequences that the dome itself claims to predict: magnetic field changes (WIN-010, WIN-025, WIN-068), gravitational anomalies (WIN-011), and ionospheric effects. These physical effects depend on the actual shadow — where photons are physically blocked — not on an apparent optical alignment. The dome cannot retreat to "it's all apparent" without abandoning the very eclipse predictions it counts as WINs.

A second defense: "OPEN-007 is acknowledged; we'll fix the moon later." But this concedes the point. The dome claims 9 eclipse-related WINs and an upcoming falsification test (2026 EBR) while its own documentation flags the moon framework as structurally broken and the eclipse prediction mechanism as "unconfirmed" (OPEN-010). You cannot claim predictive success from a geometry you admit you cannot compute.
patches.replace:
1.8 The Coordinate Collapse: When the Refraction Fix Destroys the Map

The dome's fix for the sun/firmament collision — declaring the sun's observed altitude an "optical illusion due to aetheric refraction" — invalidates the dome's own coordinate system. The same medium that bends sunlight also bends Polaris light, contaminating every position and distance the model computes. The dome's refractive indices (n = 1.2–2.8) exceed Earth's atmosphere by factors of 600–9,400, producing coordinate errors of 21–44% that the model's formulas completely ignore.

Section 1.5 documents a geometric collision: the sun's triangulated altitude (5,733 km) exceeds the firmament height at the sun's orbital radius (~4,200 km). The dome fixes this by declaring the 5,733 km figure an "optical illusion due to aetheric refraction" (raw-text/05-model.txt, line 38) and asserting the physical sun sits below the firmament ceiling. This is a reasonable instinct — if observations disagree with geometry, invoke a medium that bends light. The problem is that the dome never followed through on what this medium does to everything else.

The coordinate system assumes straight-line optics

Every position in the dome model is computed from Polaris elevation. The conversion formula is:

r(city) = solve: r × tan(θPolaris) = H(r)

where H(r) = 8,537 × exp(−r/8,619) km is the firmament height and θPolaris is the observed elevation angle. This is a straight-line formula: it assumes light from Polaris travels in a straight line from the firmament apex to the observer. The radial distance r follows from the geometric triangle — altitude divided by tangent of the elevation angle.

The V12 distance formula, the southern distance quadratic (WIN-027), the elliptical disc geometry (WIN-030), the EW angular scale factor (0.9941), and every city-pair distance validation — all of these depend on r(city) values computed from this formula.

But Polaris light travels through the same medium as sunlight

The dome's refractive index function is n(r) = 1 + 0.20 × (8,537/H(r) − 1). This gives:

Positionr (km)H(r) (km)n(r)× atmosphere
North Pole08,5371.000—
London (51.5°N)~4,3005,1971.13430×
Cairo (30°N)~6,7003,9381.23780×
Equator~10,0002,6731.441,460×
Tropic of Capricorn~12,6001,9791.662,210×
Equatorial ring14,1051,6621.832,760×
Ice wall20,0158372.846,130×

For reference, Earth's atmosphere has n ≈ 1.0003 at sea level — sufficient to produce the ~0.57° refraction that shifts the sun's apparent position at the horizon. The dome's medium is 430 to 6,130 times more refractive than our atmosphere. This is not a subtle correction; it is a medium that bends all light paths by multiple degrees.

Polaris light traveling from the firmament apex (n ≈ 1.0) to an observer at the equator (n ≈ 1.44) traverses this entire gradient. In a graded-index medium, light curves toward higher refractive index — meaning Polaris light curves outward as it descends toward the disc surface. The observer sees Polaris at a different elevation than the geometric straight-line angle. The dome's coordinate formula, which assumes the geometric angle, gives the wrong r(city).

The coordinate errors are enormous

A ray trace through the dome's own n(r) medium (using the eikonal equation for a radially-graded medium with no z-dependence — the only specification the dome provides) yields:

Observer regionn at positionGeometric θ (°)Apparent θ (°)Coordinate error
London area1.1356.649.0−1,200 km (−21%)
Mediterranean1.2344.435.7−2,800 km (−32%)
Sub-tropics1.2741.232.2−3,300 km (−34%)
Equator1.4431.421.0−5,600 km (−40%)
Southern latitudes1.6623.411.6−8,500 km (−43%)

The dome's coordinate system systematically underestimates radial distance. An observer the dome places at the equator (r = 10,000 km) is actually at r ≈ 14,000 km in the dome's own geometry, once refraction is accounted for. Every distance, every calibration, every city-pair validation computed from these coordinates inherits this error.

The 3D problem: n(r) is a 1D function for a 3D phenomenon

The coordinate error above assumes all light arrives along similar paths. Reality is worse. Refraction is inherently three-dimensional: light arriving at an observer from 30° elevation traverses a longer slant path through the high-n region than light from 70° elevation. Two observers at different positions looking at the same star see light that has traversed different n(r) profiles because the 3D geometry differs.

The dome provides n(r) as a function of radial distance on the disc — a 1D function with no z-dependence or angular specification. This means there is no defined procedure for computing the refraction of an arbitrary light ray through the medium. The coordinate formula uses a single radial mapping, but different observing geometries require different corrections. The dome has a single number (n at your r-position) being asked to correct an infinite family of different ray paths. It cannot work.

Gaia DR3 measures 1.8 billion stellar positions to ~10 microarcsecond (0.00000278°) precision from a spacecraft that observes stars at all elevation angles. The dome's n(r) at the equator has n − 1 = 0.44, which exceeds the threshold for a 10 μas displacement by a factor of 8.8 million. Any medium with n = 1.44 produces angle-dependent stellar position shifts of degrees, not microarcseconds. The dome cannot claim n(r) bends sunlight while leaving stellar astrometry untouched — a medium refracts all light.

The aetheric scaling law is spherical geometry in disguise

The deepest issue is not that the coordinate system has errors — it is why it works at all without the refraction correction. The dome's H(r) curve, the EW angular scale factor (0.9941), and the V12 distance formula collectively reproduce globe-standard geographic measurements to high precision. How?

Because they were fitted to globe-derived data. H(r) was calibrated against Polaris elevation measurements — which are, in globe terms, latitude measurements. The V12 distances were validated against great-circle distances. The EW scale was fitted to WGS84 longitudes. The exponential decay of H(r) mimics the mathematical effect of spherical curvature: on a sphere, the relationship between latitude angle and surface distance is non-linear in a way that H(r) = H₀ × exp(−r/λ) approximates.

The "law of aetheric scaling" is not an independent physical discovery — it is a fitted curve that encodes spherical geometry into a flat-disc parameterization. The coordinate system works (without refraction) because H(r) already does the work of curvature. Introducing n(r) on top of this double-counts the bending: once via H(r) (which encodes curvature implicitly) and again via n(r) (which physically bends light). The refraction fix doesn't just create an error — it reveals that the aetheric scaling law was performing geometric translation all along.

What this invalidates

Every prediction that depends on the dome coordinate system is affected:

  • All V12 distance predictions (WIN-027, WIN-030, WIN-069): r(city) values are wrong; distances computed from wrong r-values are wrong.
  • The H(r) curve itself (WIN-044, WIN-056): H(r) was derived from Polaris observations through the refractive medium. The calibration data is contaminated.
  • All angular measurements (WIN-058, WIN-065): Angular coordinates measured through n(r) >> 1 are distorted.
  • Cavity geometry predictions (WIN-001, WIN-002, WIN-038, WIN-061): Schumann resonance, Tesla frequency, and other cavity-mode predictions use cavity dimensions derived from H(r). Wrong dimensions give wrong frequencies.
  • Geomagnetic predictions (WIN-012, WIN-053, WIN-063): Magnetic field models use dome coordinates to specify measurement positions and decay functions.
  • The EW angular scale (0.9941): A fitted correction to WGS84 longitudes — it corrects the globe-to-dome mapping, not an independent dome measurement.

This is not a cascade affecting individual predictions. It is a foundational contradiction: the refraction fix that saves the sun from the firmament collision destroys the observational basis of the coordinate system that the rest of the model depends on.

The other side

The dome model deserves credit for recognizing the sun/firmament collision and attempting a physically motivated fix. Introducing a refractive medium is, in principle, the right kind of approach — it is what a physicist would try. The n(r) formula is mathematically well-defined and the apparent/physical altitude distinction is conceptually sound.

A dome defender might argue that a future version could derive a self-consistent coordinate system that accounts for n(r). This is theoretically possible — but it would require full 3D ray-tracing through the medium for every observation used to calibrate the model. More fundamentally, any such derivation that reproduces globe-standard observations from a flat disc must, mathematically, be equivalent to spherical projection. The refraction fix does not break the dome because it was implemented poorly; it breaks the dome because it reveals that the "aetheric medium" was always performing the work of curvature, and pushing n(r) to values large enough to physically relocate the sun exposes the machinery.
patches.rationale: EXP-129: Full rewrite of Section 1.8 from Refraction Cascade (cascade table approach) to Coordinate Collapse argument. New section shows dome own n(r) produces 21-44% coordinate errors. Supersedes EXP-078 cascade table. Per human directive HNOTE-REFRACT-RESTRUCTURE-001.

patches.find: Unlike classic flat-earth models that rely on visual arguments and simple discs, the ECM introduces mathematical formalism — Finsler metrics, spherical harmonics, dielectric coupling constants. This makes it testable in ways that most flat-earth claims are not. The sophistication is real; whether it survives contact with data is the question this review answers.
patches.replace: Unlike classic flat-earth models that rely on visual arguments and simple discs, the ECM introduces mathematical formalism — Finsler geometry, exponential height functions, and electromagnetic coupling constants. This makes it testable in ways that most flat-earth claims are not. The sophistication is real; whether it survives contact with data is the question this review answers.
patches.rationale: SEC-1.2 curmudgeon review: "spherical harmonics" appears NOWHERE on the dome site — this is a fabricated strawman that could undermine credibility if a dome defender exploits it. Also: "dielectric coupling constants" replaced with accurate terminology. "spherical harmonics" removed entirely.

SEC-1.2 curmudgeon review: display text says "Section 1b" but links to #section-1-5 (V6 restructure artifact). Display text should say "Section 1.5" to match the anchor it links to and the section numbering in the HTML.
